title: The One Without The Ski Trip

expressions.dialogue: Monica: Okay, let's go!! Let's **hit the road**!!
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 출발하다, 길을 나서다
expressions.nuance: Very common in everyday spoken English for departing on a trip or simply leaving. Still widely used across all age groups.
expressions.example: It's getting late—we should **hit the road** before traffic gets worse. → 늦어지고 있어—차 막히기 전에 이제 출발하자.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: It's never taken me a week to get over a relationship. / Monica: It's never taken you more than a shower to **get over a relationship**.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: (이별·실연·힘든 일에서) 회복하다, 극복하다
expressions.nuance: Versatile—'get over' works with breakups, failures, illnesses, and shocks. Extremely common in both casual and formal English.
expressions.example: It took me months to **get over** that breakup. → 그 이별에서 회복하는 데 몇 달이 걸렸어.

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: (handing Rachel a cup) Oh, hey, look, I found coffee! Okay, let's **skedaddle**.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 후다닥 자리를 뜨다
expressions.nuance: Slightly old-fashioned or comedic; used in informal, friendly situations for a playful tone. Still heard today but dated enough to sound intentionally quirky.
expressions.example: Class is almost over—let's **skedaddle** before the professor assigns more work. → 수업 거의 끝났다—교수님이 숙제 더 내기 전에 후다닥 나가자.

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: Wait, I'm not just gonna drink somebody's old coffee. / Phoebe: Okay, **your highness**.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: (비꼬아) '고귀하신 분', '까다로우신 분'
expressions.nuance: Sarcastic title for someone being overly fussy or demanding. Works naturally between close friends; can sound rude to strangers. Still used today.
expressions.example: You need the coffee at exactly 65°C? Okay, **your highness**. → 커피가 딱 65도여야 한다고? 알겠어요, 귀하신 분.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Well Ross was **hangin' out** over at our place, Rachel comes over to borrow some moisturiser from Chandler...
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: (친구들과) 어울리다, 별 목적 없이 함께 시간을 보내다
expressions.nuance: One of the most common casual verbs for spending time with others without a specific plan. Universally used in American English today.
expressions.example: We were just **hanging out** at the park all afternoon. → 우리 그냥 오후 내내 공원에서 놀았어.

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: Why? What happened now? / Joey: Yeah, you **shoulda been there** last night.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 거기 있었어야 했는데 (네가 없어서 아쉬웠다 / 뭔가 대단한 일이 있었음을 암시)
expressions.nuance: A fixed chunk signaling the listener missed something memorable or dramatic. The contracted 'shoulda' form is typical in casual speech. Still widely used.
expressions.example: The finale was insane—**you should've been there**! → 피날레 진짜 미쳤었는데—거기 있었어야 했어!

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Ross did ask us first, and we **set that night aside**.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: ~을 위해 시간·돈·자원 등을 미리 빼두다, 따로 확보해 두다
expressions.nuance: Used when reserving time, money, or resources for a specific purpose in advance. Common in both everyday and professional contexts today.
expressions.example: I've **set aside** the whole weekend to help you move. → 이삿날 도와주려고 주말 내내 비워 뒀어.

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: I'm asking you first, right?! I mean I'm **playing by the rules**.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 규칙·약속대로 행동하다, 공정하게 따르다
expressions.nuance: Emphasizes that one is following established norms or agreements, whether official or social. Can be used earnestly or with slight self-satisfaction. Still widely used.
expressions.example: I gave you the first chance to respond—I'm just **playing by the rules** here. → 나는 네가 먼저 할 기회를 줬어—그냥 규칙대로 한 거야.

expressions.expression: use humor as a defense mechanism
expressions.dialogue: Chandler: When my parents got divorced is when I started **using humour as a defence mechanism**.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 힘든 상황을 유머로 모면하다, 불안을 농담으로 가리다
expressions.nuance: A psychological concept used naturally in everyday speech to explain why someone deflects stress or discomfort with jokes. Still commonly used.
expressions.example: She laughs off every serious conversation—she **uses humor as a defense mechanism**. → 그녀는 진지한 대화마다 웃어넘겨—유머로 자신을 방어하는 거야.

expressions.expression: feel bad about / feel bad for
expressions.dialogue: Joey: Hey, does anybody else **feel bad about** Ross?
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: feel bad about: ~에 대해 죄책감·미안함을 느끼다 / feel bad for: ~가 안쓰럽다, 측은하다
expressions.nuance: 'Feel bad about' targets one's own actions (guilt/regret); 'feel bad for' targets another person (sympathy). Both are extremely common in everyday spoken English.
expressions.example: I really **feel bad for** her—she had no idea what was going on. → 그녀가 진짜 안쓰러워—무슨 일인지 전혀 몰랐잖아.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: ...unless of course I'm stepping on some toes here, in which case I can just **mosey on**, I've got plenty of people to help on the Interstate.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 천천히 (혹은 슬그머니) 자리를 뜨다
expressions.nuance: Conveys a leisurely or casual departure; here Ross uses it with light sarcasm. Informal and slightly regional (U.S. Southern/Western), but widely understood. Still occasionally used.
expressions.example: If you guys don't need me, I'll just **mosey on** home. → 필요한 사람이 없으면 나 슬그머니 집으로 가도 되겠다.

expressions.dialogue: Carol: So you can either **sulk** here for a half hour and then go pick them up, or save us both time and **sulk** in the car.
expressions.speaker: Carol
expressions.meaning: 토라지다, 시무룩하게 있다, 삐지다
expressions.nuance: Describes moodily staying silent or withdrawn after feeling wronged. Can describe children or adults, though applied to adults it carries a slightly childish connotation. Still widely used.
expressions.example: Stop **sulking** and just tell me what's wrong. → 그만 삐지고 뭐가 문제인지 말해.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Yeah, and not **put us in the middle**.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 두 사람 사이에서 어느 편인지 선택하도록 강요하다, 제3자를 곤란하게 끼워 넣다
expressions.nuance: Describes forcing a third person to take sides in a conflict. 'Don't put me in the middle' is one of the most common uses. Still widely used.
expressions.example: My parents keep arguing and expect me to choose—please stop **putting me in the middle**. → 부모님이 계속 싸우면서 내가 편들기를 바래—제발 나를 중간에 끼우지 마.

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: Can you **be civil**?
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 기본적인 예의를 지키다, 최소한의 예의 바른 태도를 유지하다
expressions.nuance: Asks someone to maintain minimal polite behavior despite personal conflict. 'Keep it civil' and 'stay civil' are close variants. Still widely used.
expressions.example: I know you two have history, but can you at least **be civil** at the dinner table? → 둘이 사이가 안 좋은 거 알지만, 저녁 식사 자리에서만큼은 예의 좀 지킬 수 있어?

expressions.dialogue: Ross: ...I got you the best one I could, 'cause that's not where you want to **skimp**.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~에 돈·노력을 아끼다, 충분히 투자하지 않다
expressions.nuance: Usually negative—implies cutting corners on something important. 'Don't skimp on X' is a very common warning pattern. 'Skimp on quality/sleep/ingredients' are typical collocations. Still widely used.
expressions.example: Don't **skimp on** sleep the night before a big presentation. → 중요한 발표 전날 밤에 잠을 아끼면 안 돼.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: Rachel, the tampons here are only a penny. Let's **stock up**.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: (물건을) 잔뜩 사두다, 미리 비축하다
expressions.nuance: Used when buying more of something than immediately needed, often in preparation. 'Stock up on X before Y' is a very common pattern. Still widely used today.
expressions.example: We should **stock up on** snacks before the road trip. → 장거리 드라이브 전에 간식을 잔뜩 사놔야 해.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Yes, yes, **it's just that** we ah, we kinda already made plans with Ross.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 사실은 ~이라서... (거절이나 불편한 설명 앞에 쓰는 완충 표현)
expressions.nuance: A softening phrase before delivering disappointing or awkward news. Signals that what follows is a genuine explanation, not an excuse. Extremely common in polite refusals today.
expressions.example: I'd love to help, **it's just that** I'm completely swamped this week. → 도와주고 싶은데, 사실 이번 주는 완전히 정신이 없어서.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: We're sorry honey. / Rachel: Oh, it's okay. (starts to leave) / Rachel: Oh, well okay. Well, **there you go**.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: (체념하며) '그렇군', '어쩔 수 없지' / 또는 '봐봐, 그게 답이야'
expressions.nuance: Highly versatile: signals resigned acceptance, confirms a point one is making, or marks the completion of an action. Very common in both British and American English today.
expressions.example: He cancelled again—**there you go**, that's your answer. → 또 취소했네—봐봐, 그게 답이야.

expressions.dialogue: Phoebe: ...you have to **figure out a way** to be around each other.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: ~할 방법을 찾다, 어떻게든 해결책을 마련하다
expressions.nuance: The chunk 'figure out a way to ~' is especially natural for problem-solving scenarios. More informal than 'find a solution.' Extremely common in spoken English today.
expressions.example: We need to **figure out a way to** finish this before the deadline. → 마감 전에 끝낼 방법을 찾아야 해.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: ...it came to about $112, but what the hell, just **call it an even** 110?
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 서로 없던 일로 하다, 비기다 / 금액을 반올림해 딱 맞게 정하다
expressions.nuance: Used to settle a small debt or score without exact accounting—implies both parties are now square. 'Let's call it even' is the most common form. Still widely used.
expressions.example: You paid last time, I paid this time—let's just **call it even**. → 지난번에 네가 냈고 이번엔 내가 냈으니까 그냥 없던 걸로 하자.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Here we go. Okay, **brace yourselves**.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 각오해라, 마음의 준비를 해라 (충격적이거나 힘든 것이 올 때)
expressions.nuance: A warning that something difficult, surprising, or dramatic is about to happen. 'Brace yourself for ~' and 'brace yourself, [something] is coming' are also common patterns. Extremely common today.
expressions.example: **Brace yourself**—the boss wants to see everyone after lunch. → 각오해—점심 후에 사장님이 다들 보고 싶다고 해.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Well, then, I **might as well** offer to stay.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 어차피 ~하는 게 낫겠다, ~해도 무방하다 (소극적 동의)
expressions.nuance: Expresses reluctant or unenthusiastic agreement—there's no strong reason not to do something. Different from 'should,' which implies obligation. Extremely common in everyday spoken English.
expressions.example: I'm already at the library—I **might as well** stay another hour and finish this. → 어차피 도서관에 왔으니 한 시간 더 있다가 끝내도 되겠다.

expressions.dialogue: Carol: You slept with another woman? / Ross: Oh, **you're one to talk**.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 당신이 할 말은 아니지, 남 말할 처지야?
expressions.nuance: A defensive retort pointing out the other person's hypocrisy—they've done the very thing they're criticizing. Slightly confrontational but natural between close friends. Still common today.
expressions.example: You're calling me messy? **You're one to talk**—have you seen your room? → 나한테 지저분하다고? 남 말할 처지야—네 방은 봤어?

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: Why would you even want to come Ross? You're a horrible skier. / Ross: Oh-oh, **hitting me where it hurts**, my ski skills.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 상대방의 약점을 찌르다, 아픈 곳을 건드리다
expressions.nuance: Describes targeting someone's most sensitive weakness—emotionally or professionally, not physically. Common in everyday conversational English today.
expressions.example: Criticizing her cooking when she's already stressed really **hits her where it hurts**. → 이미 스트레스받는 상황에서 요리를 비판하는 건 진짜 아픈 곳을 찌르는 거야.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: ...unless of course I'm **stepping on some toes** here, in which case I can just mosey on.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 남의 영역을 침범하다, 의도치 않게 남의 계획이나 권위를 방해하다
expressions.nuance: Used to politely check whether you're intruding on someone else's plans, authority, or feelings. Often used as a considerate preemptive question. Still widely used.
expressions.example: I don't want to **step on your toes**, but I had a few ideas for improving the proposal. → 당신 영역을 침범하고 싶지는 않은데, 제안서를 개선할 아이디어가 몇 가지 있어요.
